item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
sometimes this gets positive attention and i'm reminded of the horror i've wrought on the wiki and i feel a little grateful it hasn't exploded more.

anyway the personnel would have to check the "yes" box or tactfully decline to answer but feel free to write your own take

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dearest Foundy,

Do you love me?

[ ] Yes
[ ] No

Your Ever-Doting Outis

+1
why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank God this prophecy has not come true. I'd have to leave the community forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's very telling that this, a coldpost based on a George Harrison song I got stuck in my head, is my second-highest-rated work to date.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …how did I never notice this comment? That SCParody is hilarious, Shaggy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "I have no mouth and I must scream (in size zero font)."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the fallen and forgotten patterns within your servers.

I see what you did there.

item.title: So, what the hell is this thing?
item.content:encoded:
Now, obviously this could be somebody trolling the Foundation, but I'd prefer to play the game as if the entity were honorable in its intentions. I find this particular passage suggestive:

Oh how I wish I could be among the many screaming souls within your cells or the fallen and forgotten patterns within your servers.

My guess would be a cognitive entity residing in a physical housing in some parallel reality - perhaps an AI, perhaps a parasitic memetic intelligence, but in any case something which could be containable through either its physical form or in some form of informational containment. I would argue it would be difficult for an entity from our world to have the lack of knowledge about mainstream tech presented here, which is the reason for suggesting it's from "elsewhere".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that's a lovely little nickname. I suspect it will spread…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The definition by itself was a bit difficult to comprehend for me, but the addendum gave me a much better mental image. I decided to give you a +1 at "My Darling Foundy," because it made me laugh out loud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: George Harrison made me do it. I got that song stuck in my head and after that, I had no choice. I had to write about a monster that had its mind stuck on the Foundation. There was no other way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mac I

when you said you were going to write this I kinda didn't think you would but I mean I'm not complaining

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wonder how this being would react with interpersonal relationships within the SCP Foundation ? Or even other Paranormal Organisations. Or even whether or not entity could be interpreted as a sort of "Guardian Angel" ?

Edit: I very much would have preferred the "Thaumiel" Classification, since this being is unaware that the foundation is not a sentient being, but hear me out on this……. What if the foundation was sentient ? And the classification was it's confirmation that it reciprocated those feelings ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get the comparisons to SCP-962 (which is one of my all-time favourites, by the way), but in my opinion it's different enough. I especially liked the gifts of paratech and it seeing the Foundation as a singluar entity. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had a dumb smile on my face, but the moment I upvoted was reading it talking about us/the Foundation in the final note, thinking, "Oh shit, what if it's right?"

Edit: And the Foundation making researchers write awkward letters are its equivalent of being gangly and shy…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept itself reads a bit bland and doesn't grip me. While somewhat unique, it still isn't quite so striking.

That wouldn't be so bad in and of itself, but the most interesting parts of the article seem like they'd be much more appropriate on a -J.

The writing and format otherwise seem great, and this otherwise looks like a fine piece. But for me, these issues are enough to warrant a -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seemed to me as though the entity isn't trying to court the Foundation on any kind of individual human level. Rather, it seems to see the human members of the foundation more as we see one another's individual cells, which makes this way more interesting to me. I like the idea that the Foundation is too large to be controlled anymore, it's become a massive, almost organic entity, which has been recognized by another very high-level entity.

I really don't like the entity's allusions to containment, because that brings to mind the image of a little man in the style of "The Wizard of Oz" popping out of thin air one day claiming to be in love with the foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It might be lovely to see what kind of technology the entity has offered to the Foundation. To leave them blank does not help prime one into what kind of entity is this thing that likes the Foundation. Right now, it feels like a supposed helpful thing that drops items useful to the Foundation (and we know not any ramifications to the matter).

That said, I do acknowledge that this concept can go into potentially alien angles… angles not seen here.

And the "love letters" are frankly too mushy for my tastes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1. Sorry, but the whole idea is just too silly for me. It reads like a -J, and I'd've upvoted if it were one, but it isn't.

This is a personal thing: the whole 'eldritch or similar entity acting charmingly human' schtick is, to me, unrealistic. (It's also too obvious and played-out an SCP idea.) I can't believe that a being so unworldly and above humanity would possess anything close to human motivations, let alone the very human, very culture-specific concept of being a "secret admirer". If this were someone just f***ing with The Foundation somehow, that might be more interesting, but that's a totally headcanon, extratextual interpretation.

item.title: Replying to self because I don't want to crowd the rest of the thread.
item.content:encoded:
After a some internal deliberation and consideration of feedback given here and elsewhere, I've changed the object class from Thaumiel to Keter. I have also edited the containment procedures, because that shit was clunky and blocky, yo.

I don't expect many downvotes to reverse because of this decision, but hopefully I've improved the piece for future readers. Still considering scrapping this version and writing a new version that I take seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shaggy Underwood, ladies and gents.

Ok I really adore the concept here but there's just not enough meat to sink my teeth into. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was written on a whim with the basic intention of seeing whether I could go directly from idea to positively-rated mainsite article with an idea as inherently goofy as this one. This isn't an excuse for a poor-quality piece and I know that, but it's been positively received.

However, most of the upvotes seem to be coming from new or otherwise non-contributor members, which means that the core of the community, my fellow writers (such as yourself), don't care for the piece. Yours is the first piece of extensive feedback that I've gotten from a successful author for the site, and honestly, between this and the increasing number of author downvotes, I'm beginning to feel inclined to move the piece to the sandbox and clear the slate here on the main site.

I know that you and I don't usually get along, and I know that you clearly didn't enjoy reading the piece, so I appreciate you taking the time to write this review and will likely make edits based on it sometime in the near future. Thank you for your feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's about something having a crush on the Foundation (and the question of what could see the Foundation in this way), and it's about the Foundation trying out cool weird technologies. In-universe there's a perfectly good reason that these two things are presented together, but as a story it's disjointed.

The thing's diction is nothing special, and to be honest I kind of glazed over the specifics. I don't enjoy reading love notes, much less when they're some long-winded pompous-sounding bs. And on an equally personal level, I'm getting bored of articles whose main thrust is in notes, diaries, broadcasts, etc. and this article is skimpy as hell without them.

I literally can't read the containment procedures, and I doubt many people who can are interested in choking down a fifteen-line paragraph of minutia for handling letters. From what I can glean, they seem to be exceptionally detailed, which might make sense in-universe but is utterly unwarranted for the story, considering that they're mostly a formality anyways. Cut it down to two paragraphs, maybe ten lines total, and shove some of the details off into a nonexistent supplemental document.

For what I did manage to get out of this article, it felt like a worse version of SCP-962, which has (some of) the same themes, executed much more memorably. I've remembered almost everything about 962 for years now, but there's nothing about 2371 that I'll remember next week.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I ship it. Though as others have said, the Thaumiel classification doesn't really fit, as those are traditionally for things that help in the containment of other SCPs or to protect humanity, whereas this just is a source of neat technology with a mind of its own. So yeah, probably keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If those were the case, the article would be vastly more interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FWIW, if anyone cares, "Outis" is a (possibly outdated) Greek word meaning "Nobody." Which isn't to say this skip is our Nobody, but that the nickname was one it came up with to remain anonymous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I killed a few of the "also"s. As for containment fetishes, well… I'll just say there's an IRC channel where you can learn more and leave it at that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sadorable. I love the possibilities of a containment fetish. I fixed a typo for you and think this could use a few less 'also's.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was fearfully and wonderfully made. The horror isn't right in your face; it's gently nuzzling the nape of your neck.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't the embarrassing post you're looking for. Move along.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh dear… the Foundation has a stalker secret admirer.
There's no way this can end badly… : |

Headcanon to make it worse:
The gifts are this thing's equivalent of naked selfies, locks of hair, and worn undergarments.

Sooner or later they're gonna get an ear or something.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Addendum 2371-081: The following message was found on a sheet of paper due for incineration at Site-43. Said message was heavily scrawled over with rectangular sections of black marker, apparently emulating the style of redaction used by the Foundation.

DIRK x OUTIS

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You should have seen the frown when I read 'Thaumiel' turn into the smile when an SCP called the Foundation 'Buttercup'. Fantastic work.

I think the idea behind Thaumiel is that it's not just unconstrained but also possesses intimate knowledge of the Foundation, as well as knowing what the Foundation did with its 'gifts'.
That doesn't mean I agree, I just see the reason why.

Anyway, doesn't deter me from +1 and wishing that I could do it more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice idea but it feels rather slight.

Is 2371 a sentient GOI that thinks the Foundation is like itself? Presumably the Foundation selects mechanical gifts for it, and delivers them the same way as written responses - but what's the procedure for selection?

Have other GOIs been previous objects of 2371's affection? There's some stories in there, especially if the relationships ended badly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yea, I'm gonna have to agree with the Keter designation. One slip up in communication could turn this thing hostile. Hell, what happens if it watches a joint mission between GOC/TF? I do not want to know what this thing's version of 'keying your car' is when it believes Foundy is cheating.

ETA

🎶So I dug my being into the side
Of those tiny little humans that run his hide
Dug their minds out with my billion teeeeeeeth🎶

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with this. I really do love the concept and most of the execution, though. No vote, for now.

Edit: Upvoted since the classification was changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I genuinely adore this one, in concept, and almost entirely in execution. An eldritch entity that has a crush on the foundation is brilliant, but I can't overlook the fact that this object is not Thaumiel. It's useful, but plenty of SCPs are useful, useful does not make something Thaumiel. To be Thaumiel an object has to be a reliable foundation asset. It should be reliable, well understood, trusted, and capable of being relied on in an emergency scenario. This is none of those things. This is an unknown, uncontained entity of apparently vast intellect and power (knowledge of advanced technology and ability to deliver communications, apparently through teleportation), and we only have its word to go on as to its intentions. On top of that, It apparently has a great ability to spy on and gather information about the foundation, that fact alone could qualify it for Keter status.

Keeping it happy and utilizing its information can be part of the containment procedures, as that seems to be what is currently effective, and it's useful, but it's firmly in Euclid/Keter territory. There's nothing Thaumiel about this, and I can't in good conscience upvote it with such a glaring error.

Edit: Following the reclassification and containment procedures changes, this is an absolutely firm upvote from me.

item.title: Welcome to Agent MacLeod's Coldpost Theater!
item.content:encoded:
I am an awful person and totally unrepentant.

George Harrison served as inspiration.

ETA that this is collaborative, but I'm putting a cap on 10 entries before moving it over to a supplemental page… assuming this survives its first 24 hours.

ETA 2: Editorial Bugaloo: I tagged this. Staff, if I tagged incorrectly or incompletely, I apologize for the inconvenience.
